main_summary.summary: Joint oral history interview with John and Jean Rosenberg, civil rights attorneys, conducted by David Cline on August 15, 2013, for the Library of Congress and Smithsonian National Museum of African American History and Culture Civil Rights History Project. Jean Rosenberg recounts her Quaker upbringing in Abington, Pennsylvania, her education at Earlham College, and her 1965 entry into the Justice Department Civil Rights Division as a research analyst under John Doar. John Rosenberg describes his birth in Magdeburg, Germany in 1931, his family's escape from Nazi Germany on February 22, 1940, growing up in Gastonia, North Carolina, service in the US Air Force as a navigator, and his 1962 entry into the Civil Rights Division. Both detail litigation in Mississippi, Alabama, Georgia, and Louisiana including voting rights cases in Leflore County, the Fannie Lou Hamer beating case, the Hazel Brannon Smith newspaper bombing, the prosecution of Sheriff Jim Clark's 1966 Selma election manipulation under Judge Daniel Thomas, poll tax cases under the Voting Rights Act of 1965, and the James Earl Ray extradition. They describe leaving the Civil Rights Division in 1970 and founding Appalred (Appalachian Research and Defense Fund) in Prestonsburg, Kentucky, providing legal services for poverty, environmental, and Black Lung issues across 37 Appalachian counties.
key_themes: Justice Department Civil Rights Division litigation 1962-1970
Mississippi and Alabama voter registration cases
Appalachian legal services and poverty law
main_summary.historical_significance:
This interview is a primary-source documentation of the operational mechanics of the Justice Department Civil Rights Division during its formative period under John Doar from 1962 onward, told by two of its line-attorney and research-analyst staff. The Rosenbergs describe specific cases including the Bob Moses Leflore County jail negotiation, the Fannie Lou Hamer Winona beating prosecution, the Heartman Turnbow firebombing case in Holmes County, the Yazoo County 'foot Campbell' pistol-shot intimidation, the Sasser Georgia deputy sheriff prosecution, the Hattiesburg Forrest Lynd contempt case, the Houston school desegregation dot-map evidence, the 1966 Sheriff Jim Clark Selma election ballot-count case before Judge Daniel Thomas, and the James Earl Ray extradition affidavit gathering. The interview also documents the FBI-Civil-Rights-Division institutional tension and the post-1970 founding of Appalachian Research and Defense Fund (Appalred) as a model for rural legal aid expansion, including the Mud Creek Clinic and Appalachian Citizens Law Center spin-off.

The joint format provides cross-corroborating recollection of the Selma 1966 ballot-count work where Jean and Dorothy Shelton Landsberg counted 17,000 ballots under observation by Clark's deputies, the Lowest Reese grand mal seizure incident illustrating segregated emergency services, and the personal trajectory from Justice Department lawyering to Eastern Kentucky poverty law. The Rosenbergs' German-Jewish refugee and Quaker peace-witness backgrounds illuminate the motivational substrates linking Holocaust memory and Quaker social-action tradition to civil rights enforcement work.

chapters.title: Jean's Quaker Formation
chapters.summary: Jean Rosenberg describes her Philadelphia birth, adoption by a transplanted New York couple, family conversion to Quakerism at Abington meeting, and Quaker influence on her commitment to consensus decision-making and social action. She recounts her high school refusal to participate in air raid drills at Abington High School, being called a 'Communist dupe' by the principal, and being seated by the plate glass window during drills.

chapters.title: Earlham and Entry to the Civil Rights Division
chapters.summary: Jean attends Wilmington College, transfers to Earlham College in Richmond Indiana, and graduates June 1965. Through her Earlham friend Dorothy Shelton Landsberg, whose father was an IRS lawyer in Washington, she meets John Doar at a convocation. Doar invites her to apply to the Civil Rights Division. She qualifies her loyalty oath and joins as a research analyst in June 1965 working on school desegregation, police brutality, and employment cases until spring 1970.

chapters.title: Refugee from Nazi Germany
chapters.summary: John Rosenberg describes his October 7, 1931 birth in Magdeburg, Germany, his father's role as teacher and welfare agency staff in the Jewish community, and the family's experience during Kristallnacht when their building's adjacent synagogue was bombed. His father spent 11 days in a concentration camp; the family escaped via Holland and arrived in the United States on February 22, 1940 on one of the last ships before the war began. His father's brother, mother, and the Rotterdam relatives perished.

chapters.title: Growing Up Jewish in Segregated Gastonia
chapters.summary: After three years in Spartanburg, South Carolina, John's family settled in Gastonia, North Carolina. His father swept floors weekdays in a textile mill and traveled weekends to alternate rabbinical duties between Spartanburg and Gastonia. John describes Gaston County's segregated schools, the Jewish community's economic prosperity but exclusion from country clubs, FBI confiscation of family binoculars and shortwave radio during wartime, and his lack of Black friends. He notes a Gaston County voting rights case reached the Supreme Court.
related_events: Gaston County voting rights Supreme Court case

chapters.title: Duke, ROTC, and the Air Force Awakening
chapters.summary: John attends Duke University on a tuition scholarship from Gastonia trustee C.E. Park, lost after the trustee's death. He rooms with two Jewish students under Duke's Jewish quota and joins the Jewish fraternity. As a chemistry major and ROTC participant, he enters the US Air Force as a navigator, serving three years in England flying SA-16 missions during the Cold War. The pivotal moment comes when his radar operator Abe Jenkins, returning by train from Long Island in uniform, moves to the back of the train at Washington's segregated boundary, prompting John's resolve to address racial caste.

chapters.title: Law School and Joining the Civil Rights Division
chapters.summary: After a year at Rohm and Haas chemical company, John attends law school at Chapel Hill, becoming friendly with Julius LeVonne Chambers, the future civil rights attorney who was number one in their class. After law school, John spends a month and a half in the Antitrust Division waiting to interview with John Doar, who was traveling weekly to Mississippi for the James Meredith Ole Miss admission. John joins the Civil Rights Division in 1962 when there were only eight or nine lawyers, motivated by Holocaust history, Air Force integration experience, and the belief that as a Southerner he could make a contribution.
related_events: James Meredith University of Mississippi admission

chapters.title: Voting Rights Litigation in Georgia and Mississippi
chapters.summary: John describes the Division's pre-1964 work focused on voting discrimination. With Robert F. Kennedy as Attorney General and Burke Marshall, John Doar built the field-based trial approach. John's first case in Sasser, Georgia under Jerome Heilbron involved a deputy sheriff who chased Black voter-registration students from town with a pistol; the all-white jury acquitted. He transferred to Mississippi at end of 1962. The Bob Moses Leflore County arrests led to a Division lawsuit and negotiated release. John discusses photographing voting records county-by-county under FBI supervision, documenting the disparate literacy-test administration that produced the freezing-principle doctrine in Judge Frank Johnson's Macon County Alabama ruling.

chapters.title: The Fannie Lou Hamer Case and the FBI Problem
chapters.summary: John details the March 1963 Winona Mississippi arrest and beating of Fannie Lou Hamer and her companions after they stopped at a lunch counter. The Division filed a criminal information against the police officers but the FBI was reluctant to investigate. John located a Black jail trustee whom the FBI claimed could not be found; the trustee testified to mopping blood off the jail floor. The December 1963 all-white-jury acquittal preceded John's drive to Sunflower County to tell Mrs. Hamer they had lost. The chapter contextualizes the institutional tension between Southern-rooted FBI agents and Division lawyers, illustrated by the Harman Turnbow firebombing case in Holmes County and the Medgar Evers funeral confrontation defused by John Doar.

chapters.title: After the Civil Rights Act: Public Accommodations and Employment
chapters.summary: John discusses the post-1964 expansion of Division jurisdiction to public accommodations, employment, and school desegregation. He describes Greenwood theater desegregation cases including the Leflore Theatre and Paramount Theatre, the Forrest Lynd contempt case in Hattiesburg featuring Black college-faculty registration testimony chronicled in Gordon A. Martin Jr's book, the Itta Bena 'Balance Do' church mass arrest of attendees aged 15 to 80 sentenced to county-farm time, the Mississippi Burning meridian civil rights workers murder investigation under Joseph Sullivan, and the Civil Rights Division attorneys cohort.

chapters.title: Voting Rights Act and the 1966 Selma Election
chapters.summary: John describes the Voting Rights Act of 1965 abolishing literacy tests and authorizing federal examiners. The 1966 Alabama primary was the first election under the Act; Sheriff Jim Clark faced safety director Wilson Baker. After Black precincts' boxes took until 4 AM to count under new Black voting officials, white election officials impounded and excluded five Black-precinct boxes. The Division filed suit before Judge Frank Johnson for an order preserving the ballots, then before Judge Daniel Thomas in the Southern District. Jean and Dorothy counted 17,000 ballots under observation by Clark's deputies including one who gave Dorothy a 'Never' segregationist button. Judge Thomas ordered the ballots counted. The chapter also covers the Viola Liuzzo Selma-to-Montgomery March murder and the Voting Rights Act's downstream effects.

chapters.title: Bogalusa, Charlotte, and Marriage
chapters.summary: John spends a month in Bogalusa Louisiana with Lou Coutter supporting CORE's James Farmer-led testers and obtaining an injunction against the Ku Klux Klan from Judge John Minor Wisdom in his undershirt on a Saturday in New Orleans. Jean describes her fear on the Meredith March near Yazoo City when a car of men followed her and a colleague through dark streets. After the Lowest Reese grand mal seizure incident in Montgomery, where the first ambulance refused to transport Reese because she was Black, John was sent to Charlotte to investigate the bombing of Julius LeVonne Chambers' house. There with Nick Katzenbach, John called Jean in Philadelphia to propose marriage.

chapters.title: Poll Tax, James Earl Ray, and Houston Schools
chapters.summary: John describes filing the Mississippi poll tax case on the Monday after the Voting Rights Act was signed and preparing the Alabama poll tax case argued by Stephen Pollak, with Governor Wallace's highway-patrol-distributed campaign brochure entered as evidence of segregationist intent. He recounts traveling to Memphis after Martin Luther King's assassination with Nick Katzenbach to gather affidavits for the James Earl Ray extradition from England. He moves to Houston for six months to prepare the dot-map school desegregation case before Judge Ben C. Connally, plus employment cases against electrical workers in Las Vegas, steam fitters in Los Angeles, and Roadway Express.
related_events: Alabama poll tax case before Stephen Pollak
James Earl Ray Memphis affidavit gathering

chapters.title: Leaving Justice and Founding Appalred
chapters.summary: The Rosenbergs left the Civil Rights Division in 1970 after the Nixon administration's retreat on school cases and the politicization of case-filing by congressional district. A three-month camping trip with their infant Michael led to Terry Lenzner connecting them with Charleston attorneys interested in Appalachian public-interest law. After visiting Harry M. Caudill, author of Night Comes to the Cumberlands, at Whitesburg Kentucky, they settled in Prestonsburg in fall 1970. John founded Appalred (Appalachian Research and Defense Fund) covering 37 Appalachian Kentucky counties through 11 offices, addressing broad form deed, strip mining, Black Lung, food stamps, Medicaid access, and consumer law.

chapters.title: Building Appalachian Civic Infrastructure
chapters.summary: After retiring from Appalred in 2002, John founded the Appalachian Citizens Law Center focused on coal-related issues. Jean discusses statewide education advocacy work. The Rosenbergs describe difficulty of balancing environmental regulation with coal-mining employment, the inequity of out-of-state coal ownership and the Kentucky severance tax, and their community-building work including the local science museum with planetarium and the local Job Corps center advisory board. The interview closes with Jean's reflection that they have been fortunate to keep values congruent with day-to-day work.
